name: The Madamète lakes
description:
This route, without major difficulty, leads to the discovery of the Madamète lakes and their countless neighbors. From the perfectly equipped resort of Tournaboup, you set off for a spectacular immersion in the Néouvielle reserve.

The route begins at the station car park, before entering the superb Gaubie valley. There you find the GR 10 to enter this peaceful and preserved valley. Bridges and ruins bear witness to centuries-old pastoral activity as you advance. The slope gradually rises towards the Aygues Cluses refuge. Brand new, it allows you to refuel or fill up with water in a remarkable setting.

The climb continues and you quickly reach the Madamète lakes. Nestled at the foot of the mountains, their green banks contrast with the raw and rocky environment that surrounds them. A final uphill effort then leads to the Col de Tracens. The view of the Madamète lakes and the other slope rewards the efforts made to reach the highest point of the route.

The last portion of the route essentially consists of descending to return to the starting point in Tournaboup. Along the way, you discover an anthology of lakes: Nère, Tracens, Blanc and Dets Coubous. Be careful not to lose sight of the route in the rocky portions that border some of them. When you find the Gaubie valley, all you have to do is continue until the finish of the course.
region_intro: Hiking around Sers offers access to the diverse landscapes of the Hautes-Pyrénées region in France. The area is characterized by significant elevation gains, leading through mountain terrain and offering views of the Pyrenees. Trails often feature rugged paths and high-altitude environments. Best hiking trails around Sers The most popular hiking route is The Madamète lakes, a difficult 10.3 miles (16.5 km) trail that takes about 7 hours to complete. This route features substantial climbs towards alpine lakes. Another top favourite among local hikers is Hourquette de Mounicot, a difficult 12.0 miles (19.3 km) path. This trail navigates challenging mountain passes with extensive ascents. Local hikers also love the Pic du Midi de Bigorre – Col du Tourmalet loop from Col du Tourmalet, a 9.5 miles (15.3 km) trail leading through high mountain terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 25 minutes. Hiking around Sers is defined by high mountain passes, significant elevation changes, and panoramic views of the Pyrenees. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate paths to challenging ascents. The routes in Sers are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 500 reviews. More than 6000 hikers have used komoot to explore Sers's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Sers?
A: There are 65 hiking trails around Sers, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. This includes 11 easy routes, 40 moderate paths, and 14 challenging ascents.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect when hiking around Sers?
A: Hiking around Sers primarily offers access to the diverse landscapes of the Hautes-Pyrénées region in France. You can expect significant elevation gains, leading through rugged mountain terrain with panoramic views of the Pyrenees. The area is characterized by high-altitude environments and challenging paths.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for families or beginners in Sers?
A: Yes, Sers offers 11 easy hiking trails that are suitable for beginners or families looking for less strenuous options. These routes provide a gentler introduction to the region's natural beauty without the significant climbs found on more difficult paths.

Q: What are some challenging hikes around Sers for experienced hikers?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Sers offers 14 difficult routes. Notable options include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383261094/the-madamete-lakes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Madamète lakes&lt;/a&gt;, a 10.3-mile (16.5 km) trail with substantial climbs towards alpine lakes, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383273975/hourquette-de-mounicot" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hourquette de Mounicot&lt;/a&gt;, a 12.0-mile (19.3 km) path navigating challenging mountain passes with extensive ascents.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Sers?
A: Yes, there are several circular hiking routes available. One popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1609209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pic du Midi de Bigorre – Col du Tourmalet loop from Col du Tourmalet&lt;/a&gt;, a 9.5-mile (15.3 km) trail through high mountain terrain. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/36105722" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col du Tourmalet – Beautiful lake loop from Par d'Abbay&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 6.7-mile (10.8 km) route.

Q: What interesting landmarks or natural features can I see along the trails in Sers?
A: Hikers in Sers can explore various natural features and landmarks. You might encounter beautiful alpine lakes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1036163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac Dets Coubous&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also known for its prominent mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/128882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tourmalet Pass&lt;/a&gt; and viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1155634" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luz-Ardiden&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking views of the Pyrenees. Additionally, the Roc de Sers, a prehistoric site with a sculpted reproduction of a parietal frieze, is a unique attraction in the area.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Sers?
A: The trails in Sers are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 500 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the high mountain passes, significant elevation changes, and the panoramic views of the Pyrenees. The diverse terrain and challenging ascents are also often highlighted as key attractions.

Q: When is the best time of year to go hiking in Sers?
A: Given the significant elevation gains and high mountain terrain in Sers, the best time for hiking is typically from late spring through early autumn (roughly June to September). During these months, trails are generally free of snow, and weather conditions are more stable, making the challenging routes more accessible and enjoyable. Always check local weather and trail conditions before heading out.

Q: Are there any huts or refuges available along the hiking routes in Sers?
A: Yes, there are several huts and refuges in the Sers area that can provide shelter or a place to rest. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2829825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bastan Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2829827" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Glère Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5455641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refuge de Campana de Cloutou&lt;/a&gt;. These can be valuable resources, especially on longer or multi-day hikes.

Q: Is Sers in France the only hiking destination with that name?
A: While this guide focuses on Sers in the Hautes-Pyrénées, France, there are other locations named Sers that are also known for outdoor activities. For instance, Serles Mountain in Tyrol, Austria, is a renowned hiking area, and Sersheim in Ludwigsburg, Germany, offers charming trails through rolling hills and forests. Each 'Sers' provides a distinct hiking experience.

Q: Are there any specific trails that offer great viewpoints of the Pyrenees?
A: Many trails in Sers offer incredible views of the Pyrenees due to the region's high-altitude nature and significant elevation gains. Routes that lead to or near prominent features like the Pic du Midi de Bigorre or traverse mountain passes such as the Col du Tourmalet are particularly known for their panoramic vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1609209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pic du Midi de Bigorre – Col du Tourmalet loop&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent example for stunning views.
tips: Tournaboup is a small outdoor sports resort equipped with a bike park with ski lifts. For hikers, it is above all an opportunity to fill water bottles in a fountain and to have a bite to eat on the restaurant terrace while seeing the valley which descends towards the Néouvielle.
The Gaubie valley includes the Gaubie and Pountou bridges. It houses the ruins of the Gaubie cabins and leads to the Aygues Cluses plateau and its refuge.
Inaugurated in 2023, this new refuge is ideally located in the Nouévielle massif. In a preserved natural setting, it offers good meals in addition to its 35 beds.

The Madamète lakes are two magnificent bodies of water nestled in the hollow of the Néouvielle massif. The GR 10 trail passes through the middle of the two lakes where there are numerous bivouac spots.

The Coubous cabin is an open and unguarded refuge. It is located at an altitude of 2,041 meters on the edge of the eponymous lake.

There is very little wood around, so if you are planning to light a fire in the fireplace to warm up the atmosphere a little, we suggest you bring it from the valley. Which is also the general usage.

You can sleep up to 4-5 people there quite comfortably. From 6 you will have to tighten up a little, but at the same time it warms up.
